zh.lifeSummary: Allen | Bruin| Kingston出生在英國英格蘭薩默塞特。他和Joan le Caile至少有1個兒子。

zh-hans.lifeSummary: Allen | Bruin| Kingston出生在英国英格兰薩默塞特。他和Joan le Caile至少有1个儿子。

nameMeanings.name: Kingston
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English: habitational name from any of numerous places throughout England called Kingston or Kingstone. Almost all of them derive from Old English cyning, cing ‘king’ (genitive cyninges, cinges) + tūn ‘farmstead, estate’, though some have a different etymology, such as Kingston on Soar in Nottinghamshire, which derives from Old English cyne ‘royal’ + stān ‘stone’.

nameMeanings.name: Allen
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Variant spelling of   Alan  , in Britain generally found only as a surname, but in North America equally common as a given name.

nameMeanings.name: Bruin|
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Dutch: from a personal name based on Middle Dutch bruun ‘brown’, or a nickname referring to the color of the hair or complexion. Compare De Bruin and Bruyn .
English: of uncertain origin. It may, as Reaney suggested, reflect a pronunciation of French   Brun   ‘brown’.

variants.name: Brewin
meanings: Irish (Sligo): Anglicized form of Gaelic Ó Braoin ‘descendant of Braon’, a byname meaning ‘moisture, drop’.

variants.name: De Bruin
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Dutch Maarten, Gerrit, Hendrik, Annick, Cornelis, Marinus.
Dutch: variant, with the definite article de ‘the’, of Bruin ‘brown’, a nickname referring to the color of the hair or complexion.

variants.name: Bruins
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Dutch Albertus, Berend, Dirk, Gerrit.

variants.name: Bruinsma
meanings: West Frisian: patronymic, formed with the suffix -ma ‘(one of the) men of’, from a genitivized form of the personal name Bruin .

variants.name: Bruyn
meanings: 20th-century coinage from bryn ‘hill’; also a short form of Brynmor .
